meta.name: WA Police Force Corporate Writing Style Guide

sections.h:
INTRODUCTION

The Western Australia Police Force Corporate Writing Style Guide has been prepared to help WA Police Force employees prepare a range of written communication.

The material in this document is based on information from the following publications:

• The Style Manual for Authors, Editors and Printers, Sixth Edition, 2002, John

Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd

• The Western Australia Police Service Communication Manual, 1998, Western

Australia Police Academy

• The Macquarie Dictionary Revised, Third Edition, 2003.

This document should be treated as a basic guide and is by no means exhaustive.

Further information or clarification can be obtained from the Corporate Communications Branch by emailing Corporate.Communications.Branch@police.wa.gov.au

sections.h:
WRITING STYLE

Many types of written documents need to be prepared by the WA Police Force. For example:

• briefing notes

• reports

• letters

• memorandums

• research papers

• business proposals

• planning submissions

• operational orders

• stories for publications

• emails

To help you prepare these documents, this Style Guide provides the basic skills to ensure your correspondence is professional, consistent, neat and accurate.

When preparing documents, remember the following:

• Use language that readers will understand and avoid using jargon. Never assume

people know what you are talking about.

• Be precise, avoid unnecessary words and stick to the point you are trying to make.

• Vary sentence lengths and try to use an average of about 22 words a sentence.

• Avoid euphemisms, clichés and overused words/phrases.

• Be consistent with how you format the document and the style/words you use. This

ensures the document looks “clean,” is easy to understand and is presented in a precise fashion.

• Always proof-read your document at least twice and, if appropriate, ask someone

else to read it for you. Mistakes can be easily missed when you work on a document for many hours, so a second or third proof-reader will help alleviate this problem.

• Try not to rush yourself on a document. It is always safest to spend extra time having

something proof-read and thoroughly checked, than to rush it, not have it read and find something is wrong with it once published. Remember that what you are writing is not only a reflection of you, but the agency as a whole and the Commissioner of Police.

sections.h:
Abbreviations

An abbreviation is a shortened or contracted form of a word or phrase. It is usually the first letter of a word, a few other letters but not the last, and is followed by a full stop. For example:

• approx. (approximately)

• vol. (volume)

• enc. (enclosed)

• att. (attached)

• Hon. (Honourable)

• sic (thus) is used to draw attention to an error in quoted material/text

• cc. (carbon copy) is to be placed under the signature block

• etc. (et cetera) is preceded or followed by a comma only when the sentence

structure demands it

• i.e. (that is) and e.g. (for example) are both preceded by a comma but there is no

comma after them

• c/- (care of) is used in addresses

sections.h:
Acronyms

An acronym is the formation of the initial letters of words (and sometimes other words), pronounced as a single word. The full spelling must be used in the first reference, followed by the acronym. For example:

• Department of Fire and Emergency Services (DFES)

• State Emergency Service (SES)

• Queensland Police Service (QPS)
sections.b: Acronyms An acronym is the formation of the initial letters of words (and sometimes other words), pronounced as a single word. The full spelling must be used in the first reference, followed by the acronym. For example: • Department of Fire and Emergency Services (DFES) • State Emergency Service (SES) • Queensland Police Service (QPS)

sections.h:
Ampersands (&)

Ampersands should not be used except in very specific circumstances such as where it is used as part of an organisation’s name. For example:

• Cobb & Co.

• P & N Bank

• P & C Association
sections.b: Ampersands (&) Ampersands should not be used except in very specific circumstances such as where it is used as part of an organisation’s name. For example: • Cobb & Co. • P & N Bank • P & C Association

sections.h:
Apostrophes (’)

There are two main uses of apostrophes:

1. To show the omission of letters. For example:

• I couldn’t (could not) attend the meeting.

2. To indicate possession. For example:

• The student’s chair is red.

• The students’ chairs are red.

• His father’s car is red.

Do not use apostrophes for:

1. Simple plurals. For example:

• Full-time employees (FTEs NOT FTE’s)

• Non-commissioned officers (NCOs NOT NCO’s)

• Explosive detection dogs (EDDs NOT EDD’s)

2. Referring to a number of years. For example:

• During the 1980s, police officers wore khaki uniforms. (NOT During the 1980’s,

police officers wore khaki uniforms).

sections.h:
Attachments and enclosures

Attachments (att.)

Where correspondence says other documents are attached, ensure the hardcopy version is stapled or included with the reply. If an attachment is a previous letter it must be a copy of the original signed document. Ensure ‘att.’ appears after the date in the correspondence.

Enclosures (enc.)

Enclosures are generally too large to attach but are in the same envelope as the correspondence. Where correspondence states there are enclosures, ensure they are enclosed and that ‘enc.’ appears after the date at the bottom of the correspondence.

If an attachment is a previous letter it must be a copy of the original signed document. Ensure ‘att.’ appears after the date in the correspondence. Enclosures (enc.) Enclosures are generally too large to attach but are in the same envelope as the correspondence. Where correspondence states there are enclosures, ensure they are enclosed and that ‘enc.’ appears after the date at the bottom of the correspondence.

sections.h:
Brackets ( ) [ ]

Brackets are generally used to enclose material that is not essential to the meaning of a sentence.

The two common types of brackets used are:

• parentheses (round brackets)

• square brackets.

Parentheses ( )

The main function of parentheses is to enclose definitions, acronyms, comments, clarifications, additional information, etc. For example:

• Whenever police (often detectives) have attended the scene, the offenders have run

away.

Square brackets [ ]

These are used in material to show editorial insertions made by someone other than the author, to clarify something or highlight an error in the text. For example:

• Police often need to attend the High Court [which is in Canberra] which is the most

senior court in Australia.

• When Deputy Commissioner [sic] Col Blanch attended the graduation parade, he

spoke to all the new constables.

NOTE: The italicised word sic (which means thus) is used to draw attention to an error in the text that has been quoted by someone.

The two common types of brackets used are: • parentheses (round brackets) • square brackets. sections.h:
Capital letters

Sentences should always start with a capital letter. Initial capitals should also be used for proper nouns and the names of specific people, places and organisations. For example:

• Commissioner Col Blanch

• Sergeant John Smith

• Australian Federal Police

• Perth Police Station

• Newman Police Station

When referring to a specific position but without the name, you should capitalise it. For example:

• The meeting comprised the Director, Corporate Communications Branch and the

Director, Human Resources.

When referring to non-specific names/nouns, etc you do not need to capitalise. For example:

• The meeting comprised many agency directors, managers and police officers.

• One sergeant and three constables attended the meeting.

When referring to nationalities, races, tribes and inhabitants of a particular area of a country, adherents of particular religions, languages and language groups, you must use initial capitals. For example:

• Australian

• Aboriginal

• Indigenous

• Torres Strait Islanders

• American

• Chinese

When referring to a specific government, capital letters must be used for the title. For example:

• Government of Western Australia

• Commonwealth Government

However, when the words are used in a non-specific way, lower case must be used. For example:

• The role of any government is to provide the people with what they want.

• Many staff in government departments do not receive training.

• The new headquarters is at the corner of Roe and Fitzgerald streets.

sections.h:
Colons (:) and semicolons (;)

Colons (:)

Colons are most commonly used to introduce lists. If the list is the continuation of a sentence, a colon should be used. For example:

• Officers will be sent to the following locations:

Perth

o

Mandurah

o

Broome

o

Colons can also be used in the following instances:

• To separate subtitles from main titles. For example: Joe Bloggs: The true story.

• To formally introduce a quotation of reported speech. For example: In his address,

the Commissioner of Police made the following statement: “We must be vigilant.”

Semicolons (;)

A semicolon is used to link two clauses that could be treated as separate sentences but have a close link. Another use for the semicolon is to separate a series of phrases or clauses that also contain commas. For example:

• We expect ministerial approval next week; they can then start immediately.

Alternatively, to make things simpler, these statements can be joined by ‘and’ or be made into two shorter sentences.

For example: • Officers will be sent to the following locations: Perth o Mandurah o Broome o Colons can also be used in the following instances: • To separate subtitles from main titles. For example: Joe Bloggs: The true story. • To formally introduce a quotation of reported speech. For example: In his address, the Commissioner of Police made the following statement: “We must be vigilant.” Semicolons (;) A semicolon is used to link two clauses that could be treated as separate sentences but have a close link. Alternatively, to make things simpler, these statements can be joined by ‘and’ or be made into two shorter sentences.

sections.h:
Contractions

A contraction is a shortened form of a word (or words) using the first and last letters of the word. Contractions are not followed by a full stop unless they end a sentence. For example:

• Mr

• Dr

• Pty Ltd
sections.b: Contractions A contraction is a shortened form of a word (or words) using the first and last letters of the word. Contractions are not followed by a full stop unless they end a sentence. For example: • Mr • Dr • Pty Ltd

sections.h:
Dash

The purpose of the dash (-) is to indicate an abrupt or dramatic break in the text. For example:

• Now she knew who the culprit was – her son.
sections.b: Dash The purpose of the dash (-) is to indicate an abrupt or dramatic break in the text. For example: • Now she knew who the culprit was – her son.

sections.h:
Dates and times

Dates

For internal and external publications such as memorandums, formats, briefing notes, publications, media releases, etc, dates must be consistent throughout the document. For example:

• 1 January 2023

Expressions of time

The usual way to write references of time, using the 12-hour system, is with am (ante meridiem, before noon) and pm (post meridiem, after noon). For example:

• 10.30am or 12.01pm

• 10 o’clock

• 7 hours (7 hrs)

• 45 minutes (45 min)

• 36 seconds (36 secs)

When using the 24-hour system, the hours are written from 0000 hours (midnight) to 2359 hours (a full 24 hours). For example:

• 0630 hours

• 1201 hours

• 2347 hours

Shortened forms such as BC (before Christ), AD (anno Domini, in the year of our Lord), CE (of the common era) and BP (before present) should be written without full stops and with a space separating them from the year. There should always be a space between the year and the era indicator. For example:

• 125 BC

• 1788 AD

• the third century AD

NOTE: As Western Australia is in a different time zone than the rest of the country, it may be necessary to specify which time zone you are referring to. For example:

• 4.50am WST (Western Standard Time)

• 6.50am AEST (Australian Eastern Standard Time)

• 1650 WST (Western Standard Time)

• 1850 AEST (Australian Eastern Standard Time)

sections.h:
Dot points

If all of the dot points are full sentences, each must start with a capital letter and end with a full stop. For example:

Reasons for attending the course would be:

• Officers from the WA Police Force could investigate the feasibility of developing

legislated guidelines for future investigations.

• Applications for research funding could be supported in the main areas of

investigation.

• Liaising with officers from other jurisdictions to allow WA Police Force members

the chance to gain knowledge and share information.

If the dot points are arranged more like a short list, no initial capital letter is used. Leave each point without closure. For example:

The four types of assistance available are:

• monetary assistance

• equipment or environmental modifications

• resources

• advisory services
sections.b: Dot points If all of the dot points are full sentences, each must start with a capital letter and end with a full stop. For example: Reasons for attending the course would be: • Officers from the WA Police Force could investigate the feasibility of developing legislated guidelines for future investigations. • Applications for research funding could be supported in the main areas of investigation. • Liaising with officers from other jurisdictions to allow WA Police Force members the chance to gain knowledge and share information. If the dot points are arranged more like a short list, no initial capital letter is used. Leave each point without closure. For example: The four types of assistance available are: • monetary assistance • equipment or environmental modifications • resources • advisory services

sections.h:
Exclamation marks (!)

Exclamation marks/points are used at the end of a sentence to indicate surprise, emphasis, strong emotion or a command. For example:

• How dare you yell at me like that!

• Stop! Police.

Try to avoid using exclamation marks, especially in headlines.
sections.b: Exclamation marks (!) Exclamation marks/points are used at the end of a sentence to indicate surprise, emphasis, strong emotion or a command. For example: • How dare you yell at me like that! • Stop! Police. Try to avoid using exclamation marks, especially in headlines.

sections.h:
Hyphens (-)

Hyphens are mostly used to connect words to better explain following nouns or a position.

For example:

• The new school-based officers were enjoying helping students

• An 18-year-old man was charged with arson

• Officers undertake a 10-day course

• Sergeant John Smith, Officer-in-Charge, Perth Police Station

When the last letter of a single-syllable prefix (e.g. de-) is a vowel, and the word that follows begins with the same vowel, a hyphen is inserted to prevent any misunderstanding. For example:

• de-emphasise

• re-enter

• co-operate

• co-ordinate

However, many words nowadays have become common place so the hyphens are dropped. When deciding to use hyphens, remember why the words are hyphenated, i.e. to show the joining of words that have been shortened, or to join two or more words to give them one meaning. Remember to be consistent with whatever method you choose. For example:

• officer-in-charge

• up-to-date

• well-known

For example: • The new school-based officers were enjoying helping students • An 18-year-old man was charged with arson • Officers undertake a 10-day course • Sergeant John Smith, Officer-in-Charge, Perth Police Station When the last letter of a single-syllable prefix (e.g. de-) is a vowel, and the word that follows begins with the same vowel, a hyphen is inserted to prevent any misunderstanding. For example: • de-emphasise • re-enter • co-operate • co-ordinate However, many words nowadays have become common place so the hyphens are dropped. When deciding to use hyphens, remember why the words are hyphenated, i.e. to show the joining of words that have been shortened, or to join two or more words to give them one meaning. Remember to be consistent with whatever method you choose. For example: • officer-in-charge • up-to-date • well-known

sections.h:
Inquire or enquire

Inquire/inquiry and enquire/enquiry can be used interchangeably, but inquire/inquiry are the forms used by the WA Police Force.
sections.b: Inquire or enquire Inquire/inquiry and enquire/enquiry can be used interchangeably, but inquire/inquiry are the forms used by the WA Police Force.

sections.h:
‘ise’ or ‘ize’?

Often people get confused as to whether to use ‘ise’ or ‘ize’ in certain words. The Australian version is to use ‘ise’. For example:

• modernise

• organise

• recognise

As ‘ize’ is an international version (mainly American) and the only time you should use this version is when it appears in official titles/names of groups, countries, people, organisations, etc. For example:

• National Association of Police Organizations

• Polizei Berlin

NOTE: Check or update your Microsoft Word dictionary so it uses the Australian English version.
sections.b: ‘ise’ or ‘ize’? Often people get confused as to whether to use ‘ise’ or ‘ize’ in certain words. The Australian version is to use ‘ise’. For example: • modernise • organise • recognise As ‘ize’ is an international version (mainly American) and the only time you should use this version is when it appears in official titles/names of groups, countries, people, organisations, etc. For example: • National Association of Police Organizations • Polizei Berlin NOTE: Check or update your Microsoft Word dictionary so it uses the Australian English version.

sections.h:
Italics

Italics must be used for the titles of:

• Books and periodicals

e.g. From the Line, The Power of One, The West Australian

• Plays and long poems

e.g. The Man From Snowy River, The Mousetrap

• Musical compositions/songs

e.g. The Phantom of the Opera

• Films, videos, and television and radio programs

e.g. Gone With The Wind, Four Corners

• Works of art

e.g. Mona Lisa, Blue Poles

• Legislation and legal cases

e.g. Police Powers and Responsibilities Act 2000

• Names of ships, aircraft and vehicles

e.g. Titanic, Cygnet V

• Scientific names of plants and animals

NOTE: For animals, scientific names at the genus, species and subspecies levels are italicised.

With botanical names, italics are used for the genus, species, subspecies, variety, form and subform levels, e.g. such as the Cassia nemophila var. coriacea.

• Technical terms and technical terms being defined

e.g. Gouache (a method of painting)

• Letters, words and phrases being cited

e.g. Guerrilla (comes from the Spanish word meaning ‘little war’)

sections.h:
Measurements

Measurements are written in metric (Australian standard). For example:

• 12 kilometres (km) = 12 km

• 52 metres (m) = 52 m

• 29 centimetres (cm) = 29 cm

• 9 millimetres (mm) = 9 mm

• 2 tonne (t) = 2 t

• 458 kilograms (kg) = 458 kg

• 145 litres (L) = 145 L

• 897 millilitres (ml) = 897 ml
sections.b: Measurements Measurements are written in metric (Australian standard). For example: • 12 kilometres (km) = 12 km • 52 metres (m) = 52 m • 29 centimetres (cm) = 29 cm • 9 millimetres (mm) = 9 mm • 2 tonne (t) = 2 t • 458 kilograms (kg) = 458 kg • 145 litres (L) = 145 L • 897 millilitres (ml) = 897 ml

sections.h:
Monetary amounts

Currency is to be expressed as a combination of numerals and symbols. For example:

• $10,000

• $150.32

• 10c

• $10,567.25

Millions of dollars can be written as $3,500,000 or $3.5 million or $3.5m (no space is used in $3.5m).

It is only necessary to distinguish Australian currency if multiple currencies are being referred to in the one document, and for reasons of consistency, the preferred style within the WA Police Force is one recommended by the International Organisation of Standardisation (currency codes). For example:

• AUD10,000 (i.e. Aussie dollar)

• NZD2,055 (i.e. New Zealand dollar)

• EUR5,000 (i.e. Euro)

• USD25.90 (i.e. US dollar)

• SGD5.94 (i.e. Singapore dollar)

• GBP17.50 (i.e. British pound)

NOTE: European countries use a comma as a decimal point, e.g. EU 145,50 whereas in Australian documents the same amount would be shown as $145.50.

For example: • $10,000 • $150.32 • 10c • $10,567.25 Millions of dollars can be written as $3,500,000 or $3.5 million or $3.5m (no space is used in $3.5m). It is only necessary to distinguish Australian currency if multiple currencies are being referred to in the one document, and for reasons of consistency, the preferred style within the WA Police Force is one recommended by the International Organisation of Standardisation (currency codes). For example: • AUD10,000 (i.e. Aussie dollar) • NZD2,055 (i.e. New Zealand dollar) • EUR5,000 (i.e. Euro) • USD25.90 (i.e. US dollar) • SGD5.94 (i.e. Singapore dollar) • GBP17.50 (i.e. British pound) NOTE: European countries use a comma as a decimal point, e.g. EU 145,50 whereas in Australian documents the same amount would be shown as $145.50.

sections.h:
Numbers

Numbers from zero to nine are written in full, while numbers from 10 and above must be written as numerals – unless they are written at the start of a sentence. For example:

• There are nine police stations awaiting refurbishment, while 35 have been

completed.

• Staff from the Corporate Communications Branch raised more than $12,000 for

Police Legacy.

• Thirty police officers attended the crime scene.

Hyphens are used to connect numbers (up to ninety-nine) that have been written in full. For example:

• Fifty-five students attended the meeting.

• Seventy-three constables graduated today.

Hyphens should be retained when part of a larger number but the large round number does not require a hyphen. For example:

• One hundred and ninety-five officers attended the ceremony.

• Three thousand and seventy-two people marched in the rally.

A number expressed in numerals can use hyphens to join to other hyphenated words, but hyphenated numbers expressed in words should not be used as it will cause confusion. For example:

• The 35-year-old car was used in an armed robbery.

(NOT…The thirty-five-year-old car was used in an armed robbery).

Mathematical relationships

Numerals should always be used to express precise mathematical relationships, such as an equation or ratio. For example:

• 43 + 65 = 108

• 5:1

In general text, words can be used when the mathematical relationship is not exact. For example:

• Many officers – about one in five – will be promoted within three years.

Percentages

Percentages can be written in various ways. When using the percentage symbol, it should only be used with numerals, whereas ‘per cent’ can be written with both numerals AND words. For example:

• 12%

• 12 per cent

• two per cent (NOT percent)

Roman numerals

Traditionally used for the numbering of preliminary pages in books, research papers, documents, etc. or in the titles of monarchs and popes. Try to limit the use of Roman numerals, as they can be confusing. In government publications, they also can be used to signify certificate level qualifications. For example:

• Certificate I in Policing

• Certificate II in Policing

Using a space or a comma in a number

For reasons of clarity, in the WA Police Force it is preferable to use a comma (rather than a space) when writing large numbers of four digits or more. For example:

• 1,785 (not 1 785 or 1785)

• 200,000 (not 200 000 or 200000)

• $4,015.25 (not $4 015.25 or $4015.25)

Years

When referring to years in decades, do not use an apostrophe. For example:

• 1970s NOT 1970’s

• During the 80s, people started buying mobile telephones. (NOT 80’s)
sections.b: Numbers Numbers from zero to nine are written in full, while numbers from 10 and above must be written as numerals – unless they are written at the start of a sentence. For example: • There are nine police stations awaiting refurbishment, while 35 have been completed. • Staff from the Corporate Communications Branch raised more than $12,000 for Police Legacy. • Thirty police officers attended the crime scene. Hyphens are used to connect numbers (up to ninety-nine) that have been written in full. For example: • Fifty-five students attended the meeting. • Seventy-three constables graduated today. Hyphens should be retained when part of a larger number but the large round number does not require a hyphen. For example: • One hundred and ninety-five officers attended the ceremony. • Three thousand and seventy-two people marched in the rally. A number expressed in numerals can use hyphens to join to other hyphenated words, but hyphenated numbers expressed in words should not be used as it will cause confusion. For example: • The 35-year-old car was used in an armed robbery. (NOT…The thirty-five-year-old car was used in an armed robbery). Mathematical relationships Numerals should always be used to express precise mathematical relationships, such as an equation or ratio. For example: • 43 + 65 = 108 • 5:1 In general text, words can be used when the mathematical relationship is not exact. For example: • Many officers – about one in five – will be promoted within three years. Percentages Percentages can be written in various ways. For example: • Certificate I in Policing • Certificate II in Policing Using a space or a comma in a number For reasons of clarity, in the WA Police Force it is preferable to use a comma (rather than a space) when writing large numbers of four digits or more. For example: • 1,785 (not 1 785 or 1785) • 200,000 (not 200 000 or 200000) • $4,015.25 (not $4 015.25 or $4015.25) Years When referring to years in decades, do not use an apostrophe. For example: • 1970s NOT 1970’s • During the 80s, people started buying mobile telephones. (NOT 80’s)

sections.h:
Question marks (?)

A question mark signals a query or can be used to express doubt. For example:

• Did the officers follow the appropriate procedures for such a situation?

• Why were the youths attacking police officers?
sections.b: Question marks (?) A question mark signals a query or can be used to express doubt. For example: • Did the officers follow the appropriate procedures for such a situation? • Why were the youths attacking police officers?

sections.h:
Quotation marks (“ ”)

Quotation marks (otherwise known as inverted commas) are used to show direct speech and the quoted work of another author.

If there are several consecutive sentences quoted, quotation marks are used at the beginning of each paragraph with a full stop at the end and no closing quotation mark. Only close the final quote/paragraph in the quoted text, with a full stop and closing quotation mark.

To attribute many consecutive quotes to an author, you can, after the very first paragraph/quote, write “he said”. This introduces the person and also ensures there is no confusion or wrong attribution. For example:

Inspector Tony Hill, Officer-in-Charge of the Regional Operations Group (ROG) said the group was dedicated to supporting police across the Metropolitan Region.

“Formed on 19 January 2004, ROG is a highly visible, highly mobile police presence that provides tasking support; a trained response to antisocial behaviour, and provides operational support, on a priority basis, to the metropolitan area and regional WA,” he said.

“Its primary role is to be available throughout the region to provide tasking support to assist the districts to improve response times, reduce tasking loads during peak periods and improve service delivery.

“The group’s secondary role is to develop and maintain a suitable standard of preparedness to respond to antisocial behaviour and civil disorder incidents. This involves regular structured training, incorporating other units such as Mounted Section and Dog Squad, to ensure all ROG officers have the necessary skills and capability to respond.

“We have recently up-skilled all the ROG supervisors to Civil Disorder Commander level, to enable them to confidently lead our people in the field and ensure a professional and coordinated police response to these types of incidents,” Inspector Hill said.

Double or single quotation marks?

It is often debated whether to use double or single quotation marks for quotes, etc. Either style can be used, as long as they are used consistently.

NOTE: Do not chop and change between the styles within a single document.

Therefore, if you use double quotations for direct speech, then you would use single quotations for quotes within quotes. For example:

• “There were many people at the riot yelling out, ‘give peace a chance’, and no

damage was done,” Commissioner Blanch said.

sections.h:
Singular or plural?

When deciding whether a statement is singular or plural, you need to closely examine the sentence and how the nouns are used.

Singular

If the noun refers to a body or organisation, then it is singular. For example:

• The WA Police Force is going to change its policy on uniforms.

Plural

If you are referring to the noun as a group of more than one thing, then it is plural. For example:

• WA Police Force officers are going to endorse the uniform change.

Singular If the noun refers to a body or organisation, then it is singular. For example: • The WA Police Force is going to change its policy on uniforms. Plural If you are referring to the noun as a group of more than one thing, then it is plural. For example: • WA Police Force officers are going to endorse the uniform change.

sections.h:
Spelling

Always be careful when spelling words, such as names, places, buildings, etc. Never assume that what you have been given is accurate, especially if it is an unusual word. It is highly recommended that you always try to check the spelling with the source so you know you have clarified it correctly. Also, be mindful that some words are spelt differently in other countries.

Sometimes the simplest words can be misinterpreted, so try to be as thorough as possible with checking spelling in your document. In addition, be consistent – make sure that if one name is mentioned throughout a document, that it is spelt properly each time. Sometimes there can be many variations on a word, so be careful. For example:

• Pearl Harbor (NOT Pearl Harbour)

• World Health Organization

• Mr Whyte/White

• Jennifer/Gennifer

• Kasey/Casey/Kasee/Casie

• John/Jon

sections.h:
Proffer or prefer

It is incorrect to say that the WA Police Force has ‘proffered’ a charge. The correct verb is ‘preferred’ which when legally applied, means to put (charges) before a court, judge, magistrate etc, for consideration and judgement.
sections.b: Proffer or prefer It is incorrect to say that the WA Police Force has ‘proffered’ a charge. The correct verb is ‘preferred’ which when legally applied, means to put (charges) before a court, judge, magistrate etc, for consideration and judgement.

sections.h:
Titles and ranks

Titles

Titles such as Mr, Mrs, Ms and Miss are used when referring to people or attributing direct or indirect speech. For example:

• Mr Neil Smith will be attending the police concert.

Do not use a person’s title and honorific in the same phrase. For example:

• Corporate Communications Branch Director, John Smith

• NOT…Corporate Communications Branch Director, Mr John Smith

Ranks

For ranks of police officers, always spell them out in full in when used initially in written correspondence, as abbreviations can look messy and unprofessional. It is also a mark of respect for officers. For example:

• Commissioner John Smith

• Deputy Commissioner John Smith

• Assistant Commissioner John Smith

• Commander John Smith

• Superintendent John Smith

• Inspector John Smith

• Senior Sergeant John Smith

• Sergeant John Smith

• Senior Constable John Smith

• First Class Constable John Smith

• Constable John Smith

• Probationary Constable John Smith

However, sometimes using abbreviations is unavoidable, for instance, when making reference to an officer more than once in a written document, or when completing police forms where space is limited. The following abbreviations are preferred:

• DC John Smith

• AC John Smith

• Cdr John Smith

• Supt John Smith

• S/Sgt John Smith

• Sgt John Smith

• S/C John Smith

• 1/C John Smith

• Const. John Smith

• P/C John Smith

sections.h:
When preparing correspondence in the WA Police Force, some basic rules need to be followed.

Format

Correspondence should be:

• Arial font

• 12-point type

• fully justified

• printed on approved WA Police Force letterhead (where applicable).

The tone of the correspondence from the WA Police Force must reflect the premise that the writer has a right to the information sought and a right to think and feel the way he or she does. Replies must not be defensive.

The tone of the correspondence from the WA Police Force must reflect the premise that the writer has a right to the information sought and a right to think and feel the way he or she does. Replies must not be defensive.

sections.t: References to the Western Australia Police Force
sections.h:
References to the Western Australia Police Force

When the WA Police Force is referred to in externally-facing written communication, the ‘Western Australia Police Force’ is to be used in the first instance and written out in full. It is not necessary to then put brackets immediately following and abbreviate our name as (WA Police Force).

For multiple references to our name in the one piece of communication, use the ‘Western Australia Police Force’ in the first instance, and the ‘WA Police Force’ or ‘the agency’ in instances thereafter.

For internal communication, either the ‘Western Australia Police Force,’ ‘WA Police Force’ or the ‘agency’ may be used.

Never refer to our agency as the ‘Western Australian Police Force,’ ‘Western Australia Police Service,’ ‘WA Police Service,’ ‘Police Service,’ ‘WA Police,’ ‘WAPF,’ ‘WAPS,’ ‘WAPOL’ or any variations thereof in any communication, whether it be external or internal.

sections.h:
Preparation of correspondence for signature

The content of all correspondence must:

• be written in plain language

• be clear, concise, direct, helpful and polite

• respond to all issues raised in the letter received

• where appropriate, provide a specific officer’s name, position and contact details

Drafts of official correspondence must have the approval of the portfolio head or authorised officer before being sent.

sections.t: Preparation of internal memoranda and reports
sections.h:
Preparation of internal memoranda and reports

Internal memoranda and reports must be prepared as follows:

• be printed on plain paper

• the address block should be in upper case and bold and include the title of the

person or group to whom it is addressed and also the title of the author (using Arial font, 12-point type)

• subject headings are to be in lower case and bold (using Arial font, 12-point type)

• there is no closing salutation but the name and title of the responsible officer (in full

capitals) and date (day/month/year) are included at the end of the document

• when referring to names of people in the body of the memo or report, the surname of

the person should be shown in upper case. This is to limit confusion about the person’s surname (e.g. Brian JOHN).

This is to limit confusion about the person’s surname (e.g. Brian JOHN).

sections.h:
Page numbering

Documents of more than one page in length must have the pages numbered. Place the number at the bottom of the page, and centre it. In documents of more than one page, the page number should not appear on the first page.
sections.b: Page numbering Documents of more than one page in length must have the pages numbered. Place the number at the bottom of the page, and centre it. In documents of more than one page, the page number should not appear on the first page.

sections.h:
Spacing after punctuation

In typewritten (as distinct from typeset) material, it used to be customary to place two spaces after a colon, semicolon, full stop or other sentence-closing punctuation. Today however, programs for word processing and desktop publishing offer more sophisticated, variable spacing, so this practice of double spacing is now avoided as it can create distracting gaps on a page.

Today however, programs for word processing and desktop publishing offer more sophisticated, variable spacing, so this practice of double spacing is now avoided as it can create distracting gaps on a page.

sections.h:
Reference block

The reference block provides details so a document can be tracked, or it can identify other relevant documents and must:

• include the relevant reference file number (e.g. Ministerial file number, Objective file

number or Records file number)

• be written in 8-point Arial font.

sections.h:
Address block

The full name of the addressee and his/her address is always in capital and lower case letters. For example:

Mr George and Mrs Joan Smith

Smith and Smith Associates

GPO Box 6622

PERTH WA 6001

If a letter has been signed jointly by a couple, e.g. ‘John and Susan Brown’, the format of address in the reply is:

Mr John and Ms Susan Brown

NOT Mr and Ms J Brown

Addresses must not be abbreviated or contracted. The suburb and town must be in capitals in the address block. For example:

2 Adelaide Terrace

EAST PERTH WA 6004

In the address block on addressed letters, insert two spaces between the suburb and state, and the state and postcode. For example:

CARINE WA 6020 (note that this line is all in capitals)

sections.h:
Name of police station

Police station names must be spelt in full in any correspondence. For example:

• Albany Police Station

• Mandurah Police Station
sections.b: Name of police station Police station names must be spelt in full in any correspondence. For example: • Albany Police Station • Mandurah Police Station

sections.h:
Opening salutation

Use the appropriate title in the address and opening salutation. For example:

• Mr

• Mrs

• Ms

• Dr

• Professor

Always use the title used by the writer in the original correspondence. If it is not possible to identify the title, use Mr or Ms.
sections.b: Opening salutation Use the appropriate title in the address and opening salutation. For example: • Mr • Mrs • Ms • Dr • Professor Always use the title used by the writer in the original correspondence. If it is not possible to identify the title, use Mr or Ms.

sections.h:
Opening paragraph

The opening paragraph refers to the correspondence that is being responded to. When replying, the date of the correspondence and the subject matter are identified. For example:

• Thank you for your letter dated (date) regarding…

If there is no date on the correspondence, date it from when it was received at your office.

For example:

• Thank you for your letter received in this office on (date).

For example: • Thank you for your letter dated (date) regarding… If there is no date on the correspondence, date it from when it was received at your office. For example: • Thank you for your letter received in this office on (date).

sections.h:
Closing paragraph

Try to end on a positive note. The closing paragraph should thank the writer for bringing the issues to the attention of the signatory. For example:

• Thank you for bringing this matter to my attention.

• Thank you for raising your concerns with me.

• I trust that this information clarifies the situation for you

For example: • Thank you for bringing this matter to my attention. • Thank you for raising your concerns with me. • I trust that this information clarifies the situation for you

sections.h:
Closing salutation

When a letter is addressed to a person by name, such as ‘Dear Mr Jones’, the letter is signed:

Yours sincerely (without a comma)

When the addressee is not known and the letter is addressed as ‘Dear Sir’ or ’Dear Madam’ (or an impersonal approach is warranted) the usual closing salutation is:

Yours faithfully (without a comma)

sections.h:
Telephone and facsimile numbers

If telephone and facsimile numbers are to be part of a signature block they should be on one line to keep the length of the signature block to a reasonable size. Telephone and facsimile numbers should be separated by five spaces.

For example:

SERGEANT JOHN SMITH or JOHN CITIZEN

OFFICER-IN-CHARGE EXECUTIVE MANAGER

WEMBLEY POLICE STATION BUSINESS SERVICES

Ph: 9387 6777 Fax: 9383 9063 Ph: 9222 8989 Fax: 9222 8999 1 March 2023 1 March 2023

A number of versions may be used when putting telephone and facsimile numbers in signature blocks.

Local or intrastate

As telephone calls within WA no longer need the area code (08) included for WA telephone numbers, it does not need to be used in the signature block. For example:

Ph: 9222 1404 Fax: 9222 1060 Mob: 0413 701 357

Interstate

If you send a lot of emails interstate to other police jurisdictions or contacts, use the (08) area code in your signature block. For example:

Ph: (08) 9222 1404 Fax: (08) 9222 1060 Mob: 0413 701 357 International

If you are sending information overseas via email and you may need people to contact you, make sure you use the area code and international dial code in your signature block. For example:

Ph: +61 8 9222 1404 Fax: +61 8 9222 1060 Mob: +61 0413 701 357

sections.h:
Referring to acts

The title of an act of parliament should be referred to exactly. For example, an act or a section of an act is referred to as follows:

• Police Act 1892

• The Workplace Relations Act 1996 (Cth) s 33 details the powers of…

The title and year of the act is in italics. Only add the state/jurisdiction in brackets after the title if it is not a WA act.

For example, an act or a section of an act is referred to as follows: • Police Act 1892 • The Workplace Relations Act 1996 (Cth) s 33 details the powers of… The title and year of the act is in italics. Only add the state/jurisdiction in brackets after the title if it is not a WA act.

sections.h:
Using referencing or citations

Using referencing in written communication (such as certain types of reports), enables an acknowledgement to be made of the contribution of other writers or researchers in preparing your written work. Referencing respects the intellectual property rights of other writers, and avoids plagiarism. Referencing also provides supportive evidence to strengthen the claims or arguments you are presenting in your written work.

A simple example of when a reference is not required is:

“White is a popular colour for a car in Australia.”

Reasoning: This is generally widely known and accepted in Australia.

As compared with a simple example of when a reference is required:

“White is the most popular colour for a car in Australia.”

Reasoning: This is a quantifiable statement and a reference should be made to the source from which it was obtained.

References should always be accurate to allow readers the ability to trace the source of information referred to.

The Harvard Referencing System is the preferred standard to be used when referencing source material in reports. This means the author and date/year of publication (as a minimum) should be shown in brackets following the statement in the body of the report (as shown in this sentence). You can also correlate the reference to a piece of footnoted information that supports the statement, as shown at1 and2 below.

1 A referencing footnote may be inserted numerically via the References Tab in Word.

2 The author and date/year of publication should be detailed in brackets within the body of the report, or it can be shown as a footnote.

sections.t: Preparation of Ministerial and Parliamentary documents
sections.h:
Preparation of Ministerial and Parliamentary documents

The Office of the Commissioner coordinates the preparation and quality assurance of:

• Ministerial correspondence

• Advice to the Minister for Police

sections.h:
Emails are a major method of communication throughout the WA Police Force. The following guidelines outline the professional use of email by agency personnel.

Format of emails

Email messages should be formatted as plain black text on a white background using a standard font such as Arial.

Personalisation of work emails is not professional. Do not include:

• Quotations from famous people

• Animated GIFS

• Photographs or images

• Different background colours, creative fonts and text colours as they are often

distracting, cause difficulties for people with visual impairment, and can be incorrectly displayed in different email applications

At times, other details may be approved for inclusion for corporate reasons (such as promotion of a corporate event), but approval is required from the Corporate Communications Branch. Email Corporate.Communications.Branch@police.wa.gov.au

sections.h:
Corporate email signature block

All email applications can be set to automatically insert a corporate signature block that identifies the sender’s name and work details at the end of every email sent, i.e. sender’s full name, rank (if a police officer), PD number (digit number only, not the letters ‘PD’), job title, business unit, work address, telephone number, fax number and email address. It is good practice to include these details on every email sent.

In the case of employees who do not wish to have their full details stated in emails, sensitive information (such as location of work) may be omitted.

Supervisors and OICs are encouraged to monitor and attend to variations of the corporate email signature block for staff within their control.

For examples and instructions on how to set up your corporate email signature block, refer to the Western Australia Police Force Corporate Branding Style Guide, page 6.

All WA Police Force emails have the following disclaimer automatically attached:

This email message and any attached files may contain information that is confidential and subject of legal privilege intended only for use by the individual or entity to whom they are addressed. If you are not the intended recipient or the person responsible for delivering the message to the intended recipient be advised that you have received this message in error and that any use, copying, circulation, forwarding, printing or publication of this message or attached files is strictly forbidden, as is the disclosure of the information contained therein. If you have received this message in error, please notify the sender immediately and delete it from your Inbox.

If you send a lot of emails, you may be responsible for a large amount of unnecessary printing. A common element of a signature block that is recommended is:

Please consider the environment before printing this email.

sections.h:
Professional use of emails

It is essential to use appropriate language, correct spelling and punctuation in all emails sent. Do not use profanities or other socially-unacceptable language. The tone should be sincere and neutral.

Be concise - if more than a few paragraphs are required it may be better to use a memo or pick up the telephone. Do not add unnecessary attachments.

Do not use an email when dealing with conflict situations or sensitive, difficult, complex or emotional matters, as these can be misinterpreted and may lead to unnecessary friction.

Pick up the telephone, or better still, meet with the person.

Take care when forwarding emails. Email trails (copies of previous email communications) can be very long and may contain comments that were not intended for widespread viewing.

Where a response is required, aim to respond within two working days. This is particularly important when dealing with people outside the agency.

Use the ‘Automatic Replies (Out of Office)’ function for absences from the office and to advise the sender of an alternative contact or when his/her email will be read or actioned.

sections.t: Respect for others when using emails
sections.h:
Respect for others when using emails

Never send an email when angry - the tone of the message often reflects the sender’s mood, even if unintentionally.

If a matter is urgent it is best dealt with face-to-face or by telephone.

Setting unrealistic timelines or using inappropriate language in an email may be considered bullying.

Circulate copies of emails (cc) only when necessary and appropriate. If in doubt, don’t.

It is good practice when sending emails to more than one person, to ensure that everyone knows to whom the email has been distributed. Normally recipients should see the email addresses of each other. With large groups, it is a useful strategy to create a group email list, so that the name of the group shows as the recipient e.g. Project Team X, rather than 50 email addresses of the individual members.

Whenever a person is referred to by name in an email, it is polite to include them in the recipient list so they are aware of the communication and what is being said. If the intention is that the person should not see the email it is wise to mark the email ‘confidential’ so that it is treated as such.

sections.h:
Out of Office messaging

It is considered good practice for all WA Police Force personnel to place an appropriate Out of Office message on their personal email accounts – something professional, concise and polite that alerts enquirers (both within and external to the agency) that they are unavailable and who they should contact instead.

Messages can be tailored to suit each particular situation, but some simple examples are:

• Thank you for your message but I’m out of the office until Monday 16 July. Please

contact Constable Bill Brown on 9399 3399 or by email.

• Thank you for your message but I’m out of the office until Monday 11 June. If your

enquiry is urgent, please contact Sergeant Wanda White on 9488 4488 or by email.

• Thank you for your message but I’m out of the office until Monday 13 August and will

get back to you then. However, if your enquiry is urgent, please contact Inspector John Smith on 9577 5577 or by email.

sections.h:
Legal aspects of emails

Emails are not secure. Give consideration to how confidential information is transferred.

Exercise caution when sending emails with third party information (e.g. email addresses and personal details). This could be a breach of privacy if there is no consent to share the information.

The content of emails should be treated with the same care as letters printed on letterhead as they are a record of written correspondence. Be aware that emails may be accessed, even after they are deleted from a computer, and copies may need to be provided as part of legal disclosure processes.

Emails can be considered contractual agreements when a staff member sends an email and it indicates they are representing the WA Police Force.
